item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …Son of a bitch. That's embarrassing :) Edited. I also failed to give the second dimension measurement. Thank you for catching that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A 7.18 cm display would be so small!

… wouldn't it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thing is, that ruins the effect in my opinion. This thing doesn't just show you traumatic pictures because it feels like it. It shows you pictures that were the highlights of your life while also erasing those moments from your mind. The test log was notable because the object essentially lashed out at one individuals whose highlights - the love towards his family and his murder of them - conflicted with each other so harshly.

Basically, a picture frame that shows you OMG DEAD BODIES N SHIT is boring and trite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This feels like an article I upvote now, grudgingly, but will probably cancel out or change to downvote in 6-8 months time. It's a problem of having above average to excellent execution, but not being enough and only presenting one line of essentially "typical" set of results with the pictures (ie, normal, normal, mildly unsettling, normal, deeply unsettling, high-level WTFery, insanity, batshit insanity, back to normal)

I'd rather see less bombast in the picture contents, or even a series of nonsensical results, like a pregnant woman smiling while standing on a pile of corpses, with a fist coming towards her belly, and a caption like "Don't worry. Everything will be just fine. I'm here for you."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1. The idea of taking a murderer, slowly giving them amnesia, then confronting them with their crimes after they've forgotten them…wow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: upvoted T.T

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A dumb concept saved by a good execution. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Test Log MADE the SCP, I would not edit it if it were mine, exept to catch grammar errors, or to start a series of updates that tell a story. that would be risky though

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whoops, good catch. Fixed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
there's a random "foreword:" in place of a "Subject D-67394" in the experiment log.
Otherwise, the log really kicked it up a notch. I was wholly uninterested until that.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …… You, sir, have a fine and sturdy grip on the concept of tragedy, and I salute you for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Entirely accidental. I do see the similarities with 404, but not much with 393 other than being an inanimate object capable of altering memory.

An explanation of my headcanon on this article: this SCP is sapient, but only barely. It possesses rudimentary emotion and thought, but its 'memory' is continually zeroed. When active and observed, it pores through memories of the observer and experiences them. It's not malicious, and the amnesia is only an unintended consequence. Hence why the thing prefers memories of loved ones - it lets it feel that connection, albeit briefly.

Now when our unlucky D-Class views it, it gets all these wonderful memories of a father who legitimately loved his wife and kids, but ended up brutally murdering them. If memories of your best friend are a fine wine, this was spoiled meat. It could see from the memories it viewed that D-67394 was a man with inadequacy issues and a short temper, and decided to do its best to hurt him because he had ruined the only joy it had.

The captions actually tell a story. D-67394 felt inadequate about his wife, and always suspected her of seeing other men. The 'explosive' Independence Day was an argument that led to the first time he struck her. Things escalated; he began to resent not only her, but the children whom he saw as taking her side. Finally, he snapped, and murdered his wife in a rage and killed his children out of shame. SCP-1223 deliberately erased the memory of the murders first in order to torment him, but its anger bled through subtly even before it got to the 'punchline.'

Of course, this is just my interperatation as I wrote it, which I decided to leave out of the article to keep it lean and to the point. Feel completely free to disregard it if it's a bit too ridiculous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's remarkably similar to SCP-393 and SCP-404.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's probably a better way to phrase it. Made the change.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well written with a great interview. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Direct observation of SCP-1223 in a powered state is to be avoided at all cost.

I think that a Foundation document would have it as something like "Only D-Class personnel may directly view SCP-1223 while it is in a powered state".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for the interview. Seeing this in action makes it work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well that was disturbing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought about that halfway through writing it, meant to address it, then forgot. Probably for the better since I tend to run on in these. It's displaying actual photos from the subject's memory, so anyone else in the room would be safe, presumably. But you wouldn't want to stick around, in case the subject has been distracted for a few seconds too long - if you glanced over, the next thing displayed might be a plain black screen saying HELLO.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I upvoted this halfway through. Then went to upvote again after the log and couldn't. Damn you, Wikidot!
One question though, does it react differently with multiple people? Or will it only react to a single person at a time?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't think too much of it until I got to the test log. It really makes this SCP when you realise just how it works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ironically enough, that's how I wrote it. The method of thinking was sort of like a sick joke - what is the worst thing you can imagine? Obviously, to a parent, it's your kids being murdered. What's even worse than that? Murdering your own children. And what's even worse than that? Remembering how much you love them, then finding out you killed them. The rest of the SCP was written around the idea of that sort of horror, I hope not too transparently.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Quite good, the test log sent a shiver down my spine. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At first I was prepared to hate this, but the effect just works really well IMO. Good job Mr Wildt.
